---
title: "Working with the catalog"
description: "Share a catalog to GitHub, accept changes from a collaborator via pull request, and swap profiles when recovering entries"
icon: "code-branch"
headline: "A catalog is just a git repo"
---

In [Build a semantic catalog](build_a_semantic_catalog.qmd) you tagged a BSL flights model and dropped it into a local catalog. That catalog is a regular git repository — every entry, alias, and revision is a git commit. The moment you push it to a remote, anyone with access can clone it, query the model, propose changes, and run the model against their own backend.

This tutorial walks you through the collaboration loop end-to-end. You'll play both sides:

- **User A** publishes the catalog to GitHub.
- **User B** clones it, recovers the model, and proposes a new entry via pull request.
- **User A** reviews, merges, and pulls the changes back.

You'll also see how to swap the connection profile at recovery time, so a downstream user can run the cataloged expression against their own backend without modifying the entry.

## Prerequisites

You need:

- Completed [Build a semantic catalog](build_a_semantic_catalog.qmd) — the local catalog from that tutorial is the starting point here
- Xorq with BSL and DuckDB: `pip install "xorq[bsl,duckdb]"`
- Git installed locally and authenticated with GitHub (the `gh` CLI is convenient but not required)
- **git-annex** installed locally — `brew install git-annex` on macOS, `apt install git-annex` on Debian/Ubuntu, or see [git-annex.branchable.com](https://git-annex.branchable.com/install/) for other platforms

::: {.callout-tip}
### Why git-annex?
Catalog entries are content-addressed zip archives that can grow well past what git is happy storing in-tree (compiled artifacts, materialized parquet, model weights). xorq uses **git-annex** to keep that content out of the git history and in a content-addressed object store, while the metadata, aliases, and revision graph still travel through plain git. Every `xorq catalog ...` workflow assumes annex is present — `catalog.push()`, `catalog.pull()`, and `Catalog.clone_from(...)` all coordinate the git side and the annex side for you.

Recommend git-annex even when your entries are small. It costs nothing extra for a small catalog and saves you the migration when an entry eventually isn't small.
:::

## A catalog is a git-annex repo

Open the catalog directory you created in the previous tutorial and run `git log` — you'll see one commit per `catalog.add(...)` call. The entries are stored as annexed content under `.xorq/entries/` (each one a content-addressed zip the annex tracks by hash), the aliases are symlinks under `.xorq/aliases/`, and `catalog.yaml` is the plain-git index. Sharing the catalog means pushing both halves: the git history (via your remote) and the annex content (via a special remote like S3, GCS, or another git host with annex support).

That means GitHub's permission model, branch protection rules, and pull requests Just Work on a catalog — and a separately-configured object-store remote handles the entry content.

## Publish the catalog to GitHub (User A)

If you haven't already, recreate the catalog with annex enabled — the foundation tutorial used a default that auto-detects, and we want it explicit here. Wherever your catalog directory lives, point it at a stable path like `~/work/flights-catalog`:

```python
from pathlib import Path
from xorq.catalog.catalog import Catalog
from xorq.catalog.annex import LOCAL_ANNEX

catalog = Catalog.from_repo_path(
    Path("~/work/flights-catalog").expanduser(),
    init=True,
    annex=LOCAL_ANNEX,    # <1>
)
```
1. `LOCAL_ANNEX` enables git-annex without configuring a special content remote yet — content lives in the repo's local annex object store. You'll add a remote next so others can fetch it.

Now create an empty repository on GitHub (web UI or `gh repo create`) and wire it in. The very first push needs `-u` to set upstream tracking; after that, `catalog.push()` handles the git-and-annex coordination together:

```bash
# In the catalog directory
git remote add origin https://github.com/<you>/flights-catalog.git
git push -u origin main
git push origin git-annex      # publish the annex metadata branch
```

::: {.callout-note}
### Storing entry content
GitHub stores the git side of the catalog (history, aliases, manifests, the `git-annex` metadata branch). To store entry **content** so collaborators can fetch it, configure a git-annex special remote. The simplest options are an S3 bucket (`xorq catalog init --env-prefix XORQ_CATALOG_S3_`) or a GCS bucket (`--gcs`); see the catalog CLI's `init` and `embed-readonly` commands for details. For a single-host demo, a directory remote (`git annex initremote local-store type=directory directory=/path/to/store`) works without cloud creds.
:::

From here on, ongoing publishes use `catalog.push()`:

```python
catalog.push()   # git push + annex content sync
```

Verify the remote sees what you expect:

```bash
gh repo view --web   # opens the repo on GitHub
```

You should see two top-level items: `.xorq/` (where aliases and metadata live) and `catalog.yaml` (the index). Click into `.xorq/aliases/` and you'll see `flights-model.zip` — the alias from the foundation tutorial. The bytes behind that alias live in your annex special remote, not in the GitHub blob store.

## Clone the catalog (User B)

Switch hats. User B also has git-annex installed (it's a hard prerequisite for catalog consumers, not just publishers). They clone the catalog with one call — `Catalog.clone_from` auto-detects the annex branch and initializes the local annex on first run:

```python
from pathlib import Path

from xorq.catalog.catalog import Catalog

catalog = Catalog.clone_from(
    "https://github.com/<you>/flights-catalog.git",
    Path("~/work/flights-catalog-clone").expanduser(),
)

print("Aliases:", catalog.list_aliases())
# ['flights-model']
```

Or, equivalently, from the command line:

```bash
xorq catalog clone https://github.com/<you>/flights-catalog.git \
  --path ~/work/flights-catalog-clone
xorq catalog --path ~/work/flights-catalog-clone list-aliases
```

::: {.callout-note}
### What `clone` fetches (and what it doesn't)
`Catalog.clone_from(...)` pulls the git history and the `git-annex` metadata branch, but **not** the entry content itself. That keeps clones fast and lets a downstream user pull only the entries they actually need. The next step materializes the content for the entry we're about to use.
:::

User B never saw User A's Python file, never saw the original `to_semantic_table(...)` call. All they have is a git clone — and that's enough.

## Recover and query the model (User B)

Fetch the annex content for the alias we want, then recover the model the same way the foundation tutorial did. `catalog.fetch_entries(...)` resolves the alias, asks git-annex for the underlying object, and pulls it from whichever special remote was configured — all in one call:

```python
from boring_semantic_layer import from_tagged

catalog.fetch_entries("flights-model")    # <1>

flights_entry = catalog.get_catalog_entry("flights-model", maybe_alias=True)
flights_model = from_tagged(flights_entry.expr)

print(
    flights_model.query(
        dimensions=("origin",),
        measures=("flight_count", "avg_dep_delay"),
    ).execute().head()
)
```
1. No-op for plain-git catalogs; for annex-backed catalogs (everything in this tutorial) it materializes the entry zip locally so `flights_entry.expr` can read it.

The cataloged expression encodes how to fetch the underlying parquet (a `deferred_read_parquet` of the public R2 URL), so User B's machine pulls the data on first execution — no shared filesystem, no out-of-band data transfer.

## Propose a change via pull request (User B)

User B wants to publish a refined view: same model, but filtered to American Airlines only. Because the catalog is a git repo, they branch, commit, push, and open a PR — exactly like any other code change.

```bash
cd ~/work/flights-catalog-clone
git checkout -b add-aa-only-model
```

Build the new entry in Python:

```python
from boring_semantic_layer import to_semantic_table, to_tagged
import xorq.api as xo

BASE_URL = "https://pub-a45a6a332b4646f2a6f44775695c64df.r2.dev"
flights = xo.deferred_read_parquet(f"{BASE_URL}/flights.parquet", table_name="flights")

# Same model shape, restricted to AA
aa_flights = flights.filter(flights.carrier == "AA")
aa_model = (
    to_semantic_table(aa_flights)
    .with_dimensions(
        origin=lambda t: t.origin,
        destination=lambda t: t.destination,
    )
    .with_measures(
        flight_count=lambda t: t.count(),
        avg_dep_delay=lambda t: t.dep_delay.mean(),
    )
)

aa_model_expr = to_tagged(aa_model)
catalog.add(aa_model_expr, aliases=("flights-aa-only",), sync=False)
```

::: {.callout-note}
### `sync=False`
Passing `sync=False` keeps the add local — it commits to the working branch but doesn't push to the remote. You'll push the branch yourself in the next step, after reviewing the diff.
:::

Push the branch (and the annex content for the new entry) and open the PR:

```bash
git log --oneline -3                    # confirm: "add: <hash> (aliases flights-aa-only)"
git push origin add-aa-only-model       # publish the git side of the change
git push origin git-annex               # publish the new annex metadata
git annex copy --to=origin .            # publish the new entry's content
gh pr create --title "Add AA-only flights model" \
             --body  "Adds an AA-filtered view of the flights model under alias flights-aa-only."
```

::: {.callout-tip}
### `catalog.push()` does the same dance
Once your branch is set up, `catalog.push()` performs the git push and the annex content sync in one call. The raw-git form above is shown so the steps are visible — for steady-state use, prefer the catalog API.
:::

User A reviews the PR on GitHub. Because each `catalog.add(...)` is a single commit, the diff is small and readable: a new annex pointer under `.xorq/entries/`, a new alias symlink in `.xorq/aliases/`, and an update to `catalog.yaml`. The actual entry bytes live in the annex remote — GitHub never holds them, but the metadata it does hold is enough for any reviewer to inspect what changed.

## Pull the merged change (User A)

Once the PR is merged, User A pulls. `catalog.pull()` brings the git history up to date and refreshes the annex metadata branch; `fetch_entries(...)` materializes the new entry's content from the annex remote when you actually need it:

```python
catalog_a = Catalog.from_repo_path(Path("~/work/flights-catalog").expanduser(), init=False)
catalog_a.pull()

print("Aliases now:", catalog_a.list_aliases())
# ['flights-model', 'flights-aa-only']

catalog_a.fetch_entries("flights-aa-only")    # materialize the new entry
```

The same alias is now visible everywhere the catalog is cloned. Anyone with access can recover and query `flights-aa-only` exactly the way they recover `flights-model`.

## Swap the profile at recovery time

The catalog stores expressions, not connections. When User A built the entry they used some default xorq backend; when User B recovers it, they may want to execute against a different profile — perhaps a DuckDB session they've configured locally, or a Postgres instance with extra resources.

A **profile** in xorq is a named connection configuration: `con_name` plus connection kwargs, serialized to disk. Save one with `Profile.from_con(con).save(alias=...)`, load it with `Profile.load(...)`.

```python
from xorq.vendor.ibis.backends.profiles import Profile
import xorq.api as xo

# Capture User B's preferred connection as a named profile
local_con = xo.connect()                      # any xorq-supported backend
Profile.from_con(local_con).save(alias="local_dev", clobber=True)

# Later — possibly in a different script — load the profile and bind the entry to it
profile = Profile.load("local_dev")
expr = catalog.load("flights-model", con=profile.get_con())

print(expr.execute().head())
```

`catalog.load(name, con=...)` returns the cataloged expression bound to whichever connection you pass. The entry on disk is unchanged; the swap is purely a runtime decision.

::: {.callout-note}
### `catalog.load` vs `from_tagged`
`from_tagged(entry.expr)` rebuilds the BSL `SemanticModel` so you can call `.query(...)` against it — that's the right tool when you want the semantic-layer interface back. `catalog.load(name, con=...)` skips the BSL layer and gives you the underlying Xorq expression bound to a connection of your choosing — that's the right tool when you want to redirect execution to a specific backend without touching the entry. They compose: profiles for the connection, BSL for the dimensions and measures.
:::

## What you learned

- A xorq catalog is a git-annex repo: plain git for history, aliases, and metadata; git-annex for content-addressed entry storage. Both publishers and consumers need git-annex installed.
- Sharing it is `git push` + `git annex copy --to=origin` (or just `catalog.push()`); cloning is `Catalog.clone_from(...)` followed by `catalog.fetch_entries(...)` to materialize the entries you actually want.
- Collaboration uses the GitHub workflow you already know: branch, commit, push, open a PR. Each `catalog.add(...)` is one commit, and the diff is just metadata — content stays in the annex remote.
- `from_tagged(flights_entry.expr)` recovers the BSL model on the consumer side — exactly the same call as in the foundation tutorial, regardless of where the entry came from.
- `catalog.load(name, con=...)` rebinds a cataloged expression to a different connection at recovery time. Combined with named `Profile`s, downstream users can pick their own execution backend without modifying the entry.

## Next steps

- [Your first build](your_first_build.qmd) — package the cataloged model into a portable build artifact.
- [Switch backends](switch_backends.qmd) — see what kinds of profiles xorq supports.
- [Explore caching](explore_caching.qmd) — keep the recovered query path fast for downstream users.
